content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

SUPERSEDED IN PART
BY 1998 AMENDMENTS TO
REGULATION 18530

October 13, 1994

Marguerite P. Battersby
City Attorney
Brunick, Alvarez & Battersby
1839 Commercenter West
P.O. Box 6425
San Bernardino, CA  92412

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-94-303

Dear Ms. Battersby:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice regard-
ing the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹
regulating publicly funded mass mailings.  Since your advice
request is general in nature, we are treating your request as one
for informal assistance.²

     Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.  In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.  The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTIONS

1.  May a city newsletter that is paid for with a combination
of public funds and advertising revenue include advertising from
candidates expressly advocating the candidate's election to
office?

_______________

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento, CA  95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-94-303
Page 2


     2. May a city newsletter that is paid for with a combination
of public funds and advertising revenue include advertising from
incumbent officeholders expressly advocating the officeholder's
election to office?

                              CONCLUSIONS

     The city newsletter paid for with public funds may not sell
advertising space to incumbents or candidates expressly advocating
the candidate's election to city office.

     Moreover, the newsletter may not sell any advertisements that
feature an elected officeholder affiliated with the city.  This
would be the case whether the advertisement expressly advocated
the incumbents election or not.

                                FACTS

     According to the facts provided in your letter and by City
Manager Sam Racadio in our telephone conversation of October 13,
1994, the City of Highland has formerly published a city newsletter
called the Viewpoint with city funds and resources.  Approximately
one year ago, the city contracted with the Highland Chamber of
Commerce to have the chamber actually produce the Viewpoint, in
addition to performing other promotional duties for the city.

     You stated that the city pays for the costs of distribution
and contributes toward other costs of production.  The chamber of
commerce controls the content of the newsletter, consistent with
the requirements of the contract between the city and the chamber
of commerce.  The chamber of commerce also sells advertising in
the Viewpoint at a nominal price, the revenue from which is used
to cover the remaining costs of the newsletter and as additional
funds of the chamber of commerce.

     Recently, candidates for city office and incumbent
officeholders have sought to purchase advertising space in the
newsletter to expressly advocate their candidacies.  You have
become concerned that such advertising included in a city
newsletter could violate certain provisions of the Act.

                               ANALYSIS

Section 89001

     Section 89001 prohibits the use of public funds for certain
mailings which include the name or other reference to an incumbent
elected officer.  Regulation 18901(a) interprets Section 89001 to
prohibit any mailing if all of the following apply:

          (1)  Any item sent is delivered, by any means,
          to the recipient at his or her residence, place of
          employment or business, or post office box.  For

Our File No. A-94-303
Page 3


purposes of this subdivision (a)(1), the item
delivered to the recipient must be a tangible item,
such as a videotape, record, or button, or a
written document.

(2) The item sent either:

(A) Features an elected officer
affiliated with the agency which produces or
sends the mailing, or

(B) Includes the name, office,
photograph, or other reference to an elected
officer affiliated with the agency which
produces or sends the mailing, and is prepared
or sent in cooperation, consultation,
coordination, or concert with the elected
officer;

(3)(A) Any of the costs of distribution is
paid for with public moneys; or

(B) Costs of design, production, and
printing exceeding $50.00 are paid with public
moneys, and the design, production, or
printing is done with the intent of sending
the item other than as permitted by this
regulation.

(4) More than two hundred substantially
similar items are sent, in a single calendar month,
excluding any item sent in response to an
unsolicited request and any item described in
subdivision (b);

Regulation 18901(a).

Your facts indicate that the newsletter will be delivered to
recipients at the recipient's residence, place of employment,
business, or post office box; more than 200 will be sent in a
calendar month; and that the advertisements in the newsletter will
feature incumbent city officeholders. Moreover, public funds
will be used for the distribution of the newsletter and will be



3 A mass mailing "features an elected officer" when the item
mailed includes the elected officer's photograph or signature, or
singles out the elected officer by the manner of display of his or
her name or office in the layout of the document, such as by
headlines, captions, type size, typeface, or type color.

Our File No. A-94-303
Page 4


contributed toward the production of the newsletter.  Thus, the
newsletter will be a prohibited mass mailing under Section 89001.4

Section 85300

Section 85300 prohibits the use of public funds for the
purpose of seeking office. We have interpreted this section to
prohibit the spending of public moneys to advocate or promote a
candidate's election to public office. (Gatling Advice Letter,
No. I-90-048.)

You stated in your letter that candidates and incumbents have
asked to purchase advertising space for the purpose of advocating
the candidates' election to city office. The express intent of
Section 85300 is that candidates for office should not be assisted
in their elections with public funds. The newsletter is a
publicly funded mailing and will reach a large portion of the
voting public. By including advertising that expressly advocates
the election of candidates, the newsletter will violate Section
85300. This prohibition would apply to the inclusion of
advertisements for incumbents and challengers alike.5

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.6

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
     Counsel, Legal Division


4  Please note, if the newsletter is prepared or sent in
cooperation, coordination, or concert with the
elected officer, the newsletter may not include the name, office,
photograph, or other reference to affiliated elected officers.

5  Even where an expenditure is not prohibited by either Section
85300 or 89001, other provisions of law outside of the Act may
still prohibit the expenditure. (See, Penal Code Section 424;
Stanson v. Mott (1976) 17 Cal.3d 206.) Nothing in this letter
should be read as authorizing any specific expenditure by a state
or local government agency under laws outside of the Act.

6  Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
